title: The Shorter GRE Stamina Reset: How to Maintain Precision in a 118-Minute Sprint
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The old GRE was a marathon. You had four hours, multiple breaks, and time to recover between sections. The shorter GRE, introduced in 2024, is a sprint. The total testing time is now under two hours. The scheduled break between sections has been eliminated entirely. You move from one section directly into the next without stopping. The clock does not pause between sections. Once you begin, you are in continuous performance mode until the test ends. This format change has significant implications for your stamina, your focus, and your scoring. The students who treat the shorter GRE like the old GRE and expect to recover mid-test are the ones who fade in the final sections. Here is how to train your endurance for a continuous high-stakes sprint.

Why the Shorter Format Changes Everything

On the old GRE, the break served as a cognitive reset. You could use the restroom, drink water, eat a snack, and clear your mind before the next section. The break was not just physical recovery. It was psychological recovery. You processed the previous section, let go of mistakes, and recalibrated for what was ahead. That reset is now gone.

Without a break, fatigue compounds. The cognitive load of the first quant section carries into the first verbal section. The frustration of a difficult Reading Comprehension passage lingers into the next quant section. There is no natural point to reset. Your brain must sustain peak performance across multiple section types without interruption. This is a different skill than the old GRE required, and it demands specific training.

The shorter format also changes the pacing pressure. With fewer questions total, each question carries more weight. A single careless error on the shorter GRE has a larger impact on your final score than it did on the longer version. You cannot afford the kind of mental drift that a longer test would absorb. Precision must be maintained from the first question to the last.

How to Train for Continuous Performance

Your practice must replicate the test-day experience. When you take a full-length practice test, do not pause between sections. Do not check your phone. Do not get up from your desk. Treat the entire test as a single continuous block, exactly as it will be on test day. The first time you do this, you will notice a drop in focus around the third or fourth section. That drop is exactly what you are training to eliminate.

Build your stamina gradually. Start with two consecutive timed sections without a break. Then three. Then a full five-section test. If your focus fades, note when it happens. Was it during the second quant section? The final verbal section? The specific timing of your fatigue tells you where you need to concentrate your stamina training.

In the weeks before your test date, schedule your practice tests at the same time of day as your official GRE. If your test is at nine in the morning, train your brain to perform at nine in the morning. This means waking up at the same time, eating the same breakfast, and beginning your practice at the exact hour. Circadian rhythm alignment is real. Your cognitive performance peaks at the times your body expects to perform.

How to Manage Energy During the Test

Your physical state affects your cognitive performance more than you realize. Hydration is critical. Dehydration impairs attention and working memory within minutes. Drink water in the hours before the test. Use the restroom immediately before entering the testing room. During the test, you will not have a break to drink. You must be hydrated before you start and comfortable throughout.

Nutrition matters. Eat a meal with protein, complex carbohydrates, and healthy fat about two hours before your test. Avoid sugar and simple carbohydrates immediately before the test. They cause an insulin spike that will crash during your testing window, leaving you foggy and unfocused halfway through. Caffeine is useful if you are accustomed to it, but do not experiment with a new caffeine routine on test day. Whatever you normally consume, consume that. Test day is not the day for variation.

Between sections, when the screen transitions, you have roughly sixty seconds before the next section begins. Use this time strategically. Close your eyes. Breathe slowly. Roll your shoulders and neck. The physical reset is not as effective as a full break, but it is better than staring at the screen waiting for the clock to start. Let go of the previous section intentionally. Whatever happened in quant, good or bad, is over. The verbal section ahead is unaffected by it. Your only job now is the next question in front of you.

If you feel your focus drifting during a section, take a ten-second reset. Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths. Open your eyes and refocus on the screen. This micro-break costs ten seconds but can save you minutes of unproductive staring at a passage you are not absorbing. The time is an investment in restored attention. Use it when you need it.

How to Prevent Post-Test Collapse During the Test

The most dangerous moment in the shorter GRE is the final section. You are tired. You can see the finish line. Your brain wants to coast. This is exactly when careless errors multiply. The student who relaxes in the final section gives back points they earned in the earlier sections.

Combat the end-of-test letdown by reframing the final section. It is not the last thing you have to get through. It is the most important section because it is the one you are in right now. Every question in the final section counts as much as every question in the first section. The finish line is an illusion. There is no finish line until the clock expires. Stay locked in until the screen goes dark.

If you finish a section with time remaining, use it to review. Do not submit early just to move on. The time between sections is not a break. It is an opportunity to check your work and catch the errors that fatigue produces. A single corrected answer in the final section can be the difference between your target score and a retake.

The Bottom Line

The shorter GRE demands continuous peak performance without the recovery break that the old format provided. Train for this by taking full-length practice tests without pausing between sections. Build stamina gradually. Align your practice schedule with your test-day time. Manage your physical state with hydration and nutrition before the test. Use the brief section transitions for micro-resets. Fight the end-of-test letdown by treating the final section as the most important one. The shorter GRE rewards the students who can maintain precision from the first question to the last.
